coredata.dc:title: Highly luminescent graphene core N-doped carbon nanodots prepared under spatial nanoconfinement
coredata.dc:creator: Mikhraliieva, Albina
Nazarkovsky, Michael
Aucelio, Ricardo Queiroz
Xing, Yutao
Zaitsev, Volodymyr
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-dopped carbon nanodots (CNDs) with an extremely high quantum yield of photoluminescence (up to 95%) have been obtained by thermal treatment of citric acid embedded in the porous structure of silica with immobilized N-[3-(Trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ethylenediamine. Spatial isolation of molecular precursors in nanoreactors permits the preparation of high-quality nanoparticles with spheroidal geometry and size 2–5 nm. The particles obtained at 110–180 °C for 60–600 min demonstrate excitation-independent photoluminescence at 453 nm. HRTEM reveals that the CNDs demonstrate ordered crystalline structures of graphic materials, indicating the catalytic effect of the nanoreactor. The high-resolution XPS experiment proves the N-doping of CNDs with 4.0 at.% of nitrogen atoms incorporated into the graphene core as pyrrolic and ternary fragments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanoreactor
Carbon nanodots
Photoluminescence
Spatial isolation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of silica gel with covalently immobilized fragments of ethylenediamine
Preparation of SiO2-EDA with embedded CA
Synthesis of silica nanocomposite with embedded CNDs
Extraction of CNDs from SiO2-EDA@CNDs nanocomposite
Optimization of CA loading on SiO2-EDA

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the use of facilities of the Brazilian Nanotechnology National Laboratory (LNNano), a part of the Brazilian Centre for Research in Energy and Materials (CNPEM). The XPS and AFM facilities staff is acknowledged for their assistance during the projects XPS-20221071 and AFM-20220999. The authors are grateful for the assistance in electron microscopy studies from the Brazilian Center for Research in Physics, CBPF (LabNano). The authors acknowledge financial support from Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico (CNPq), Fundação Carlos Chagas Filho de Amparo à Pesquisa do Estado do Rio de Janeiro (FAPERJ) and Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior (CAPES). Zaitsev is thankful for the financial support from CNPq (315125/2021-7) and FAPERJ (E-26/200.939/2021, E-26/210.449/2021, E-26/010.000978/2019, E-26/010.1556/2019). Aucelio thanks scholarship form CNPq (306108/2021-6) and FAPERJ (E-26/200.810/2021, SEI 260003/001187/2020). Mikhraliieva thanks FAPERJ (E-26/201.511/2021) for the post-doctoral scholarship. Nazarkovsky is thankful to FAPERJ (E-26/202.400/2021).

The authors acknowledge the use of facilities of the Brazilian Nanotechnology National Laboratory (LNNano), a part of the Brazilian Centre for Research in Energy and Materials (CNPEM). The XPS and AFM facilities staff is acknowledged for their assistance during the projects XPS-20221071 and AFM-20220999. The authors are grateful for the assistance in electron microscopy studies from the Brazilian Center for Research in Physics, CBPF (LabNano). The authors acknowledge financial support from Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico ( CNPq ), Fundação Carlos Chagas Filho de Amparo à Pesquisa do Estado do Rio de Janeiro (FAPERJ) and Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior (CAPES) . Zaitsev is thankful for the financial support from CNPq ( 315125/2021-7 ) and FAPERJ ( E-26/200.939/2021 , E-26/210.449/2021 , E-26/010.000978/2019 , E-26/010.1556/2019 ). Aucelio thanks scholarship form CNPq ( 306108/2021-6 ) and FAPERJ ( E-26/200.810/2021 , SEI 260003/001187/2020 ). Mikhraliieva thanks FAPERJ ( E-26/201.511/2021 ) for the post-doctoral scholarship. Nazarkovsky is thankful to FAPERJ ( E-26/202.400/2021 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of N-doped carbon nanodots synthesis: Step 1 - preparation of SiO2-EDA; Step 2 - preparation of SiO2-EDA/CA; Step 3 – thermal treatment leading to the formation of SiO2-EDA/CNDs; Step 4 – Elution of CNDs.
SEM images of SiO2-EDA/CA-I (a), SiO2-EDA/CA-II (b), and SiO2-EDA/CA-I washed with diethyl ether (c).

Normalized emission spectra of SiO2-EDA/CNDs under excitation at 360 nm for the samples obtained at different experimental conditions (continues lines), and deconvolution of the emission band (dotted lines) for SiO2-EDA/CNDs-100-60 (red) and SiO2-EDA/CNDs-170-600 (black). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Impact of deconvoluted peaks on the intensity.
Surveys XPS spectra of SiO2-EDA (a), SiO2-EDA/CA-I (b) and SiO2-EDA/CNDs-150-600 (c).
High-resolution C1s (a–c) and N1s (d–f) XPS of SiO2-EDA (a,d) SiO2-EDA/CA (b,e) and SiO2-EDA/CNDs (c,f) and their deconvolution curves (dotted lines).
High-resolution C1s and N1s XPS of CNDs-150-600.

(a) Low magnification TEM image of the CDs-150-600. (b) high-resolution TEM image showing the crystalline structure of the CNDs. (c) AFM image and height profiles of the line scans marked in the image. (d) XRD pattern of the CNDs.
UV-VIS spectra of water suspension of CNDs from SiO2-EDA/CD-180-600 after 1 day and 1 year after synthesis.
Digital photo of water suspension of CNDs-150-600 in ambient light and under a beam of white visible radiation by flashlight and under UV mercury lamp.

Photoluminescence spectra of water suspension of (a) CNDs-150-600 and (b) CNDs-180-600 particles, emitted under excitation at 245–400 nm, and (c) correlation between wavelength of the excitation (λexc) band and the maximum of emission band (max λem). d) Integrated PL intensity of CNDs-150-600 (square) and CNDs-180-600 (sphere) water suspensions at different pH.
The atomic composition and corresponding binding energy of C1, N1s, O1s and Si2p in silica composites and resulted CNDs.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: XPS of this sample was obtained from a thin film of CNDs supported on the silicon wafer, and thus signal from silicon shall be neglected.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Quantum yield (QY, %) of photoluminescence from CNDs at different pH of water suspension.

head.ce:title: Highly luminescent graphene core N-doped carbon nanodots prepared under spatial nanoconfinement

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-dopped carbon nanodots (CNDs) with an extremely high quantum yield of photoluminescence (up to 95%) have been obtained by thermal treatment of citric acid embedded in the porous structure of silica with immobilized N-[3-(Trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ethylenediamine. Spatial isolation of molecular precursors in nanoreactors permits the preparation of high-quality nanoparticles with spheroidal geometry and size 2–5 nm. The particles obtained at 110–180 °C for 60–600 min demonstrate excitation-independent photoluminescence at 453 nm. HRTEM reveals that the CNDs demonstrate ordered crystalline structures of graphic materials, indicating the catalytic effect of the nanoreactor. The high-resolution XPS experiment proves the N-doping of CNDs with 4.0 at.% of nitrogen atoms incorporated into the graphene core as pyrrolic and ternary fragments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) is currently one of the most discussed classes of nano-dimensional objects demonstrating bright photoluminescence (PL), high biocompatibility and colloidal stability in aqueous systems [1–9]. Due to facile preparation and promising characteristics, the subject has been actualized in more than three thousand publications each year, Fig. S1. CDs demonstrate prospective biomedical applications [10,11], beneficial properties in analytical chemistry for detecting various pollutants [12,13], food additives and antibiotics [14]. After a decade of intensive research in synthesis and characterization, it was concluded that, in fact, CDs are a set of luminescent nanomaterials having essentially different morphology and chemical composition [15]. Based on their morphology, the class of CDs can be divided into two subclasses: (i) graphene quantum dots (GQDs), represented by single-layer disk-shaped particles with graphene basal plane; and (ii) a family of carbon nanodots (CNDs) comprised by small (usually less than 10 nm) spherical particles [16]. Contrary to GQDs, CNDs can be obtained from common organic compounds or wastes through their controlled pyrolysis or in hydrothermal synthesis, with the potential of synthesis scaling. Also, CNDs tend to present higher than GQDs quantum yield (QY) of photoluminescence, and thus more attention has been paid to the development of CNDs over GQDs [1,2,10,12,13].

Since photoluminescence is crucial for the application of CNDs, much effort has been paid to increase the quantum yield of PL [17–19]. It was demonstrated that the PL properties of CNDs essentially depend on the condition of the particle synthesis since carbon atoms in CNDs can be arranged in various allotropic forms, from highly ordered graphene-type structures to disordered carbonized polymers [20,21]. For example, it was demonstrated that the extension time of the hydrothermal synthesis affects luminescence intensity [22]. Raising the pyrolysis temperature leads to the formation of graphene-type structures in CNDs and declining QY, probably due to the annealing of the graphene core with a corresponding decrease of the core defects. Also, the size of CNDs is inversely related to their PL [23].

The essential improvements in PL properties of CNDs were achieved in 2006 when carbon nanoparticles were “passivated” by modification with diamine-terminated oligomeric poly(ethylene glycol) [24]. Later, it was established that the use of nitrogen-containing compounds in the precursor mixture enhances the QY of the resulting CNDs [25,26]. For instance, CNDs obtained from CA and primarily amines can exhibit a superior QY, up to 80–90% [25,27]. To understand the reason for such drastic changes in the photochemical behavior of CNDs, their chemical structure and photoluminescent mechanism involved were thoroughly studied [28,29]. Particularly, it was demonstrated that N-doping of CNDs improves their PL because of the introduction of pyrrolic (N-5), pyridinic (N-6) and N-quaternary (N-Q) defects in the core of CNDs [30]. But, it was also found that the hydrothermal treatment of CA with ethylenediamine resulted in the synthesis of molecular side-product – 5-oxo-1,2,3,5-tetrahydroimidazo-[1,2-α]-pyridine-7-carboxylic acid (IPCA) which can essentially contribute to the QY of as-prepared CNDs [28,29]. It turns out that preparation of highly luminescent CNDs still suffers from weak points: (a) low-temperature synthesis is beneficial to produce particles with high QY, but it leads to the formation of disordered carbonized polymers; (b) increasing the pyrolysis temperature enhances the yield of CNDs with graphene core, but leads to the accidental formation large CNDs having lower QY; (c) doping of CNDs with nitrogen increases QY of the particles but also resulted in the formation of side-products. Therefore, obtaining highly luminescent CNDs having graphene core and specified size needs further development.

Recently new approach allowing better control of the CNDs size and composition was proposed [31]. This approach is based on the spatial isolation of molecular precursors in porous [32–34] or layered [35,36] matrixes, which restricts particle growth and permits their quantum confinement. Various carriers have been tested as nanoreactors, such as organic and metal-organic frameworks [34,37], mesoporous sieves [32], layered inorganic compounds [35,36]. In addition to better control of the particle size, spatial isolation essentially improves the luminescent properties of CNDs [33,38,39]. Also, the confined space of nanoreactors can increase the probability of reaction collision and the mass transfer of reactive chemical molecules, thereby significantly improving the efficiency of chemical reactions [40]. In our previous work, it was demonstrated that mesoporous silicas have appropriate properties of the host for spatial isolation of CNDs precursors [41]. As a proof of concept, it was shown that covalent immobilization of primary amines on silica surface allowed the preparation of N-doped CNDs having excitation-independent PL [42]. The current research is a further development of the nanoreactor approach in which N-doped CNDs having an extremely high yield of photoluminescence (up to 95%) were obtained and investigated. The approach allows simple preparation of the nanoparticles having spheroidal geometry in the range of 2–5 nm and graphitic crystalline structure of the core.

In this research, toluene, N-[3-(Trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ethylenediamine (TMS-EDA) and spherical silica gel (SiO2) with a pore diameter of about 9 nm, pore volume of 1.2 cm3 g−1 and specific surface area of 400 m2 g−1 were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich (USA). The citric acid (99.5%) from Proquimios (Brazil), sodium hydroxide (99%) from Dinamica (Brazil), diethyl ether (98%) from Isofar (Brazil), and sodium bicarbonate (99%) from Synth (Brazil) were used without further purification. Toluene was used after treating with calcium hydride and following distillation. The aqueous solutions were prepared using deionized water from PURELAB Classic (UK) system. All reagents were used without further purification.

PHS-3E pH-meter equipped with BioTrode electrode from Hamilton (USA) was utilized for measuring water solution acidic conditions. Textural properties of silica gels were determined from low-temperature nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms measured on ASAP 2020 from Micromeritics (USA) and analyzed with the modified Nguyen-Do method [43]. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of CNDs were collected on FTLA-2000 spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Nicolet, USA). UV-VIS spectra of the solutions were measured on a Cary 100 spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA). Photoluminescent spectra of liquid samples were recorded using LS 55 spectrometer from PerkinElmer (UK) in a 1 cm quartz cuvette. PL spectra of solid samples were registered on a QuantaMaster 30 spectrofluorometer (Photon Technology International, USA). The morphology of the samples was studied by means of a low-vacuum JSM-6490LV scanning electron microscope (SEM) from JEOL (Japan). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using JEOL JEM 2100F microscope operating at 200 kV. The samples for TEM were prepared by drop-casting of CNDs suspension in ultrapure water onto an ultrathin carbon-coated copper grid and allowing the suspensions to dry in air. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) analysis was conducted on MultiMode 8 microscope (Bruker, Germany). A Si-tip with a force constant of 0.4 N m−1 at the resonance frequency of 70 kHz was used in the tapping peak force method. The samples for AFM were prepared via a single drop-casting method on a clean mica and dried in nitrogen flow for 2 h. Statistical analysis of the thickness distribution was performed using Gwyddion 2.53 software. The surface composition of the samples was determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using a Kα X-ray photoelectron spectrometer from (Thermo Fisher Scientific, UK) equipped with a hemispherical electron analyzer and an aluminum anode X-ray source (Kα = 1486.6 eV), providing an energy resolution of about 1 eV. The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the CDs product was collected at room temperature using an X'Pert Pro (Panalytical, UK) diffractometer, equipped with a PIXcel one-dimensional hybrid pixel technology position-sensitive device detector and operated with Ni-filtered Cu Ka radiation (l = 1.54178 Å).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of silica gel with covalently immobilized fragments of ethylenediamine
ce:section.ce:para: Silica gel with immobilized moieties of ethylenediamine (SiO2-EDA) was prepared at the first step of the synthesis by treatment of the silica with corresponding silane, Fig. 1 .

Briefly, 10 g of commercial SiO2 was purified and hydroxylated by treatment in HNO3 (3 mol L−1). Then it was dried in a vacuum at 200 °C for 4 h and, finally, suspended in 100 mL of toluene. Thereupon, 2.0 mL of TMS-EDA was added to the suspension, and the reaction mixture was refluxed for 24 h at constant stirring. After cooling down, the solid phase was separated by filtration, washed in a Soxhlet apparatus with dry toluene for 24 h and dried in a vacuum at 120 °C for 12 h. The concentration of ethylenediamine moieties was determined from XPS and by pH-titration of SiO2-EDA as 0.95 mmol g−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of SiO2-EDA with embedded CA
ce:section.ce:para: In order to produce CNDs, SiO2-EDA was saturated with CA, obtaining the SiO2-EDA/CA, Fig. 1. In a one-cycle approach, 1.0 mL of CA aqueous solution (1.55 g mL−1) was added to 1.00 g SiO2-EDA and after several minutes of mechanical shaking an excess of CA was removed from SiO2-EDA/CA by rubbing the solid with filter paper. Then, physically adsorbed water was removed from the outer silica surface under nitrogen flow. Finally, SiO2-EDA/CA was rinsed with diethyl ether (one-three times for 4 mL) to remove excess of CA adsorbed on the outer surface of silica. The obtained product was denoted as SiO2-EDA/CA-1. In a two-cycle approach 1.0 mL of CA solution was added to dry SiO2-EDA/CA-I, and the procedure used for the preparation of SiO2-EDA/CA-I was applied for one additional cycle. The product was denoted as SiO2-EDA/CA-II. The sample of SiO2-EDA/CA-III was prepared similarly to SiO2-EDA/CA-II with one additional treatment cycle.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of silica nanocomposite with embedded CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: The nanocomposites of SiO2-EDA with CNDs embedded in the porous structure (SiO2-EDA/CNDs) were obtained by thermal treatment of SiO2-EDA/CA in a nitrogen atmosphere between 100 and 180°С for 15–600 min, Fig. 1. The samples were denoted as SiO2-EDA/CNDs-X-Y, where X – the corresponding temperature of treatment (°С) and Y- is the treatment time (min).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of CNDs from SiO2-EDA@CNDs nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: To remove CNDs from SiO2-EDA/CNDs samples, they were washed out with water in a Soxhlet apparatus for 48 h (Fig. 1), with further evaporation of the suspension in a vacuum at 0 °C till dry. The resulting light-yellow powder of CNDs was labeled as CNDs-X-Y, re-dispersed in water, and stored at 4 °C. The nanoparticle suspension in water at the concentration of 1.0 mg mL−1 was prepared by ultrasonication of the corresponding powder and further filtration through a hydrophilic PVDF membrane with a pore size of 0.22 μm.
A list of all samples prepared in the research and their abbreviations are presented in Table S1.

The QYs of CNDs in diluted water suspensions were determined using quinine sulfate (QS) in 0.1 M H2SO4 as a reference through single-point and multiple-point methods [44]. In the single-point method, both absorbance and photoluminescence emission (under excitation at 350 nm) were measured for the diluted samples having less than 0.01 absorbance at λ = 350 nm. The quantum yield of CNDs (Qs) was calculated from equation (1) as follows:
(1)
Q s
=
Q R
×
I S
I R
×
A R
A S
×
η S 2
η R 2
,
where
Q R
is quantum yield of QS;
I S
and
I R
are integrated fluorescence intensity of CNDs and QS, respectively;
A S
and
A R
the absorbance of CDs and QS in water at λ = 350 nm;
η S
and
η R
are the refractive indexes of the suspension of CNDs and QS solution, respectively.
The integrated fluorescence intensity was determined as an area under the emission spectrum using SpectraGryph 1.2 software.

For the multiple-point method, solutions with different concentrations of QS and CNDs were prepared in the absorbance range of 0.01–0.05, and integrated photoluminescence intensity was measured for each solution. In this case, the value of Qs was calculated from equation (2):
(2)
Q s
=
Q R
×
K S
K R
×
η S 2
η R 2
,
where KS and KR are the slopes of the plot of the integrated photoluminescence intensity of CNDs and QS at 350 nm against their absorbance. For both methods, the following constants were used: Qs = 54% and
η S
=
η R
= 1.33.

As previously demonstrated [41], the correct preparation of the matrix with incorporated precursor is a crucial aspect of CNDs synthesis. It is essential to prepare SiO2-EDA/CA having citric acid allocated in the porous silica but not on the external surface of the particles. To reach this objective, textural characteristics of SiO2-EDA, such as specific surface area, pore size and total pore volume, were determined from N2 adsorption-desorption experiment (Fig. S2). The loading capacity of SiO2-EDA to CA was evaluated accordingly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of CA loading on SiO2-EDA
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare SiO2-EDA, silica gel with a big total pore volume (1.25 cm3 g−1) has been selected to ensure the high support capacity toward the precursor. Silica with large pores (9.0 nm) was chosen for two reasons: the pore diameter is reduced upon silanization, and silicas with smaller pores can irreversibly capture embedded CNDs [45]. Silica gel pretreated at 200 °C and anhydrous toluene were used for SiO2-EDA synthesis to ensure monolayer covalent immobilization of TMS-EDA [46], Fig. S3. Successful covalent immobilization of TMS-EDA on silica surface was confirmed by solid state 13C NMR (Fig. S4), where expected chemical shifts of the silane were identified [47].

Knowing the pore volume of silica (1.25 cm3 g−1) and solubility of CA in water (1.5 g in 1 mL), the maximum capacity of the SiO2-EDA toward CA was estimated as 1.9 g/g. To create conditions for homogenous saturation of SiO2-EDA, a slight excess of CA solution (1.5 mL per 1 g of SiO2-EDA) was used in the experiment. Since CA was used in excess, some parts of CA can be adsorbed by the outer surface of silica. Indeed, SEM images of SiO2–EDA/CA obtained in one and two cycles of saturation indicated the presence of melted CA on the outer surface of the silica, Fig. 1a–b.

The crystals of CA can be clearly seen in both cases, favoring one treatment cycle. In order to remove CA excess from the outer surface of SiO2-EDA/CA-I, diethyl ether has been used. Due to sufficient solubility of CA in ether and limited miscibility with water, it was assumed that the selected solvent would clean the external surface of SiO2-EDA/CA without essential penetration into silica pores saturated with water solution of CA. Fig. 1c shows an indication that the proposed procedure ensures purification of the external surface of SiO2-EDA/CA and high loading of CA inside the pores. The latter was substantiated with the EDS analysis: only a slight decrease (15%) in carbon content was detected for as-prepared and ether-washed SiO2-EDA/CA-I, Fig. S5. The results of the XPS analysis also confirm the presence of CA in the pores of the SiO2-EDA/CA (see section 3.3). The optimization experiment indicated that a one-step treatment of SiO2-EDA with citric acid is sufficient to saturate the porous support. Thus, samples of SiO2-EDA/CA-I rinsed with diethyl ether were used in all further experiments.
The formation of CNDs was monitored from solid-phase photoluminescence spectra of the samples obtained under thermal treatment of SiO2-EDA/CA-I.

As can be seen, SiO2-EDA/CA-I starts to emit visible light with a maximum of 460 nm under thermal treatment of the samples at 100 °C, Fig. 3. The temperature raising (to 130 °C) results in a redshift of the emission band maximum from 460 to 490 nm. The further increase in temperature (up to 180 °C) does not influence the spectral position of the emission band, Fig. 3. Thus, the results of PL indicate the formation of CNDs from CA encapsulated in SiO2-EDA starting from 100 °C. This behavior of SiO2-EDA/CA can be explained by the known effect of nanoreactors that can decrease activation energy of the chemical reaction due to the high local concentration of the reagents in the confined space of nanoreactors that can increase the probability of reaction collision and the mass transfer of reactive chemical molecules [48]. Another important observation is the weak dependence of the half-width of the luminescence band from the time of SiO2-EDA/CA thermal treatment. Slight broadening of the long-wave shoulder is only observed after 10 h of SiO2-EDA/CA pyrolysis at 180 °C. This effect demonstrates the peculiarities of nanoreactor synthesis, which restricts the growth of CNDs particles and permits their quantum confinement over a wide temperature range. The PL spectrum of SiO2-EDA/CNDs can be deconvoluted into four Gaussian peaks, which can be attributed to the photoluminescence of CNDs having slightly different sizes [49] or chemical composition [50]. The position and half-widths of the individual peaks (the fitting parameters are presented in Table S2.) remain constant for any SiO2-EDA/CNDs samples, Fig. 2
. This fact allows us to follow the changes in the peak relative intensity versus temperature and time of SiO2-EDA/CA treatment and, hence, to determine conditions leading to the reproducible synthesis of CNDs. The results demonstrated in Fig. 4 show that all samples of SiO2-EDA/CNDs obtained in the temperature range of 110–180 °C for 60–600 min presented about the same photoluminescent characteristics.
The chemical compositions of nanocomposites were studied from their XPS spectra, Fig. 5 .

The quantitative results of XPS from SiO2-EDA, SiO2-EDA/CA and SiO2-EDA/CNDs summarized in Table 1 allow us to confirm the formation of encapsulated CNDs under thermal treatment of SiO2-EDA/CA. Indeed, according to XPS analysis, SiO2-EDA contains about three carbon atoms for each nitrogen atom (C/N = 3), which is very close to atomic composition in immobilized EDA fragment (–C3H6–NHC2H4NH2), Fig. S3. After saturation of SiO2-EDA with CA, the ratio C/N increases. Apparently, the atomic content of carbon and oxygen in SiO2-EDA/CA sample also increases. Thermal treatment of SiO2-EDA/CA leads to partial decarboxylation of the encapsulated citric acid with the formation of CNDs. This process is accompanied by a visible decrease in oxygen content of the resulting SiO2-EDA/CNDs, and the atomic ratio C/N also decreases from 12 to 7.3, Table 1.

The C1s high-resolution XPS spectra of the samples can be deconvoluted into Gaussian bands that are assigned to C–C/CC (285 ± 0.2 eV), C–N/C–O (286.4 ± 0.2 eV), >CO (287.6 ± 0.4 eV) and O–CO (286.4 ± 0.2 eV) functional groups [51], Fig. 6 . Thermal treatment of SiO2-EDA/CA resulted in a pronounced decrease in relative intensity of the band at 286.4 ± 0.2 eV, confirming decarboxylation of citric acid encapsulated in SiO2-EDA/CA with the formation of CNDs, Fig. 6c.

Analysis of the high-resolution N1s spectrum provides further detail of the decomposition of citric acid encapsulated in SiO2-EDA/CA, Fig. 6d. The XPS profiles of pristine SiO2-EDA have a single dominant component (92%) with the maximum at 399.5 eV, attributed to –NH2 groups [52,53]. Saturation of SiO2-EDA with CA results in protonation of amines, which can clearly be seen in XPS of SiO2-EDA/CA, where the peak at 401.9 eV attributed to quaternary ammonium groups dominates (82%), Fig. 6e. A smaller component of the deconvoluted spectra at 400.5 eV is supposed to be attributed to citric acid N-alkyl amide, which is formed after CA immobilization onto the SiO2-EDA surface, Fig. S3. The N1s XPS of SiO2-EDA/CNDs demonstrates essential changes in the chemical state of the nitrogen atoms, as compared to SiO2-EDA/CA, Fig. 6f. The spectrum represents two major components with maxima at 400.3 and 401.3 eV, most probably corresponding to pyrrolic (N-5) and ternary (N-Q) nitrogen in N-doped CNDs, respectively [52].

According to the results of full-scan XPS, CNDs contain about 4 at. % of nitrogen (Table 1), indicating the formation of nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticles. Based on the concentration of ethylenediamine on SiO2-EDA (0.95 mmol g−1) and CA maximum loading (1.9 g/g), the maximum atomic ratio C/N in SiO2-EDA/CA can be calculated as C/N = 28.6. The results of XPS analysis presented in Table 1 show that C/N in the CNDs is equal to 12, indicating the notable decomposition of encapsulated CA. The C1s high-resolution XPS of CNDs reveals the components commonly observed in XPS of carbon nanodots obtained during the conventional procedure by pyrolysis of nitrogen-containing organic precursors, Fig. 7 .

The spectrum can be deconvoluted into four components assigned to C–C/CC, C–N/C–O, >CO and O–CO (286.4 ± 0.2 eV) groups. The N1s high-resolution XPS of CNDs contains two components at 400 eV (67.4%) and at 401.7eV (32.6%) attributed to pyrrolic (N-5) and ternary (N-Q) nitrogen, commonly present in N-doped CNDs [54].
The size and morphology of CNDs were investigated by TEM and AFM microscopy. TEM images of CNDs confirmed their spherical morphology with a mean diameter of about 2–5 nm, as shown in Fig. 8 .

HRTEM reveals that the CNDs obtained at 150 °C in nanoreactors already demonstrate ordered crystalline structures with interplanar distances of 0.24 nm, which correspond to (100) plane in graphic materials. The formation of crystalline CNDs in the porous structure of SiO2-EDA/CA at such low temperature indicates the catalytic effect of the nanoreactors on CNDs synthesis, which can be explained by high local concentration of the precursors in a small volume of the pore.

AFM images of CNDs demonstrate that the particles are well dispersed on the hydrophilic surface of mica with minimum agglomeration. The result indicates the hydrophilic nature of CNDs surface and confirms high loading of carboxylic fragments of the particle boards, which ensures their sustainability in water media. According to the AFM image, the height of CNDs varies from 1.5 to 3.0 nm, indicating their spheroidal geometry with axes in the range of 1.5–4.5 nm.

The powder XRD pattern has a broad peak centered at 2θ = 23.2° and can be attributed to the graphite crystal plane from highly disordered carbon materials (002) [9] with crystallite size about 1.2 nm determined from Debye-Scherrer equation. The peak position indicates the presence of a multilayer graphene core in CNDs with an interplanar spacing of about 0.39 nm, which is larger than that for graphite (0.34 nm) [55]. The increase in d value potentially revealed an increase in heterogeneous nature attributed to the introduction of more nitrogen- and oxygen-containing groups.

As the CNDs obtained using the nanoreactor approach consisted of crystalline N-doped CNDs having nitrogen atoms integrated into the graphene nuclei of the particle (N-Q) and peripherical N-5 sites. Both these sites can contribute to the increase of QY in CNDs, enhancing the conjugation degree and thus increasing the electron transition probability from the ground state to the lowest excited singlet state [18]. Also, the carboxylic groups at the edges of the nanoparticles ensure a high surface charge of the particles and their dispersibility in water. The UV-VIS and PL spectra recorded for the same sample after one day and one year of synthesis demonstrate equivalent results, Fig. 8.

Water suspension of the CNDs has several adsorption bands in the UV region of spectra at λ = 345, 290 and 220 nm that can be attributed to the n → π* transitions in the N- or O- containing aromatic fragments and π → π* transitions of the graphene core [56]. The UV-VIS absorption spectra of CNDs suspensions in water are persistent for at least 12 months (Fig. 9 ), which demonstrates the colloidal stability of the nanoparticles in water media and confirms the absence of IPCA in the suspension since this compound is unstable [28].
Photoluminescence of the CNDs is extraordinarily strong and observable even in the daylight, Fig. 10 .

As opposed to CNDs obtained under the convenient procedure [25], CNDs prepared in the current research demonstrate an excitation-independent emission at λ = 453 nm, Fig. 10. Moreover, PL spectra of CNDs obtained from SiO2-EDA/CA for 1–10 h at 100–180 °C are almost identical. This fact indicates that the selected procedure allowed the preparation of CNDs with a narrow distribution of the particles by size and equivalent surface chemistry in a broad experimental condition, simplifying the protocol of their reproducible synthesis.

To calculate the QY of CNDs, the integrated PL intensity of their water suspensions was determined from spectra at different pH. It was found that the correlations between IS and pH demonstrate maximum: at pH 5.0, for CNDs-180-600 and at pH 7.5, for CNDs-150-600, Fig. 11 . This variation might reflect the slightly different chemical composition of CNDs synthesized at different temperatures. In nitrogen-doped CNDs obtained at a lower temperature, N-5 functional fragments dominate, while those obtained at a higher temperature have both N-Q and N-5 fragments [54]. Also, CNDs-180-600 might have a higher degree of decarboxylation. It can be assumed that the attenuation of IS in acidic media is related to the process of protonation of N-containing fragments in CNDs, which decreases the electronic density of the graphene core [57]. Deprotonation of N-5 and N-Q centers in neutral media enables the integration of nitrogen pairs to the graphene core, resulting in increased IS. Since deprotonation of N-5 and N-Q fragments occurs at different conditions, the maximum value of IS for CNDs-180-600 and CNDs-150-600 is observed at different pH.
The QY of PL for CNDs was calculated from equations (1) and (2) using single and multiple-point methods. The results of the calculation are presented in Table 2 .

Similar to IS, the QY of CNDs essentially increased with pH from 10% (pH = 2.0) up to 95% (pH = 8.0) and then slightly (for less than 10% of maximum QY) decreased at basic pH, Table 2. CNDs obtained under the nanoreactor approach exhibit significantly higher QY than those obtained in the conventional procedure [57].

Exhitation-independent highly luminescent N-doped CNDs can be obtained by low-temperature pyrolysis of SiO2-EDA saturated with CA. According to XPS analysis, about 12 at.% of carbon atoms are present in the form of carboxyl fragments situated on the particle border and about 46 at. % establish the particle core. The rest of the carbons generate oxygen defects of the core. According to XPS spectra, about 33% of nitrogen atoms are allocated at the particle core in the form of N-Q fragments, and about 67% are present in the form of N-5 sites. Both these sites can contribute to the QY of CNDs, enhancing the conjugation degree and thus increasing the electron transition probability from the ground state to the lowest excited singlet state.

TEM image of CNDs confirmed their spheroidal geometry with axes in the range of 2–5 nm. Excitation-independent character is attributed to formed particles with narrow size distribution. HRTEM reveals that the CNDs obtained at 150 °C in nanoreactors mainly have ordered crystalline structures with interplanar distances typical for multilayer graphene. The formation of crystalline CNDs at such low temperature indicates the catalytic effect of the nanoreactors on CNDs synthesis, which can be explained by high local concentration of the precursors in a small volume of the pore.

Albina Mikhraliieva: Methodology, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Michael Nazarkovsky: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Ricardo Queiroz Aucélio: Writing – review & editing. Yutao Xing: Writing – review & editing. Volodymyr Zaitsev: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the use of facilities of the Brazilian Nanotechnology National Laboratory (LNNano), a part of the Brazilian Centre for Research in Energy and Materials (CNPEM). The XPS and AFM facilities staff is acknowledged for their assistance during the projects XPS-20221071 and AFM-20220999. The authors are grateful for the assistance in electron microscopy studies from the Brazilian Center for Research in Physics, CBPF (LabNano). The authors acknowledge financial support from Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico (CNPq), Fundação Carlos Chagas Filho de Amparo à Pesquisa do Estado do Rio de Janeiro (FAPERJ) and Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior (CAPES). Zaitsev is thankful for the financial support from CNPq (315125/2021-7) and FAPERJ (E-26/200.939/2021, E-26/210.449/2021, E-26/010.000978/2019, E-26/010.1556/2019). Aucelio thanks scholarship form CNPq (306108/2021-6) and FAPERJ (E-26/200.810/2021, SEI 260003/001187/2020). Mikhraliieva thanks FAPERJ (E-26/201.511/2021) for the post-doctoral scholarship. Nazarkovsky is thankful to FAPERJ (E-26/202.400/2021).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Optical properties of carbon dots: a review

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced removal and detection of benzo[a]pyrene in environmental water samples using carbon dots-modified magnetic nanocomposites

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Yang, S.K. Tammina, X. Li, Y. Yang, Enhanced removal and detection of benzo[a]pyrene in environmental water samples using carbon dots-modified magnetic nanocomposites, Ecotoxicol. Environ. Saf. 170 (2019) 383-390.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red-emissive carbon dots for fingerprints detection by spray method: coffee ring effect and unquenched fluorescence in drying process

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Chen, J.S. Wei, P. Zhang, X.Q. Niu, W. Zhao, Z.Y. Zhu, et al., Red-Emissive Carbon Dots for Fingerprints Detection by Spray Method: Coffee Ring Effect and Unquenched Fluorescence in Drying Process, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces. 9 (2017) 18429-18433.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoluminescence suppression effect caused by histamine on amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots with the mediation of Fe3 +, Cu2 +, Eu3 +: Application in the analysis of spoiled tuna fish

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.A.T. Toloza, S. Khan, R.L.D. Silva, E.C. Romani, D.G. Larrude, S.R.W. Louro, et al., Photoluminescence suppression effect caused by histamine on amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots with the mediation of Fe3 +, Cu2 +, Eu3 +: Application in the analysis of spoiled tuna fish, Microchem. J. 133 (2017) 448-459.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rational design of large flat nitrogen-doped graphene oxide quantum dots with green-luminescence suitable for biomedical applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Nazarkovsky, A. Mikhraliieva, C.A. Achete, L.A. Alves, J. Araujo, B.S. Archanjo, et al., Rational design of large flat nitrogen-doped graphene oxide quantum dots with green-luminescence suitable for biomedical applications, RSC Adv. 12 (2022) 14342-14355.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Successful entrapment of carbon dots within flexible free-standing transparent mesoporous organic-inorganic silica hybrid films for photonic applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Vassilakopoulou, V. Georgakilas, N. Vainos, I. Koutselas, Successful entrapment of carbon dots within flexible free-standing transparent mesoporous organic-inorganic silica hybrid films for photonic applications, J. Phys. Chem. Solids. 103 (2017) 190-196.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Branched polyethylenimine-functionalized carbon dots as sensitive and selective fluorescent probes for N-acetylcysteine via an off-on mechanism

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Liu, R.S. Li, J. Zhou, C.Z. Huang, Branched polyethylenimine-functionalized carbon dots as sensitive and selective fluorescent probes for N-acetylcysteine via an off-on mechanism, Analyst. 142 (2017) 4221-4227.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Citric acid based carbon dots with amine type stabilizers: pH-specific luminescence and quantum yield characteristics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Meierhofer, F. Dissinger, F. Weigert, J. Jungclaus, K. Muller-Caspary, S.R. Waldvogel, et al., Citric Acid Based Carbon Dots with Amine Type Stabilizers: pH-Specific Luminescence and Quantum Yield Characteristics, J. Phys. Chem. C. 124 (2020) 8894-8904.
